elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the matter-antimatter properties of elementary systems, modeled as free quantum fields, within the global structure of de Sitter spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-002: By leveraging the distinctive causal and analytic properties of de Sitter spacetime, we propose that matter-antimatter asymmetry could emerge as an observer-dependent effect shaped by time orientation within a local causal patch, rather than as a fundamental property of de Sitter Universe itself.
elements.sent-2411-003: This kinematic perspective complements, rather than replaces, standard dynamical processes — such as baryon number violation, $CP$ violation, and nonequilibrium processes — that fulfill Sakharov’s criteria.
elements.sent-2411-004: Within this framework, the limited presence of antimatter in our predominantly matter-filled Universe, specifically within the causal patch of de Sitter spacetime under consideration, may arise from these mechanisms, though through pathways distinct from conventional interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-005: An antiparticle ($CP$) behaves as the time-reversed ($T$) counterpart of a particle, following the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this Letter, building on this foundational perspective (specifically, the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation), we investigate the matter-antimatter characteristics of elementary systems, modeled as free quantum fields, within the global structure of $d$-dimensional de Sitter (dS$^{ _d$) spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Geometrically, the most illustrative representation of dS$^{ _d$ is as a one-sheeted hyperboloid embedded in a $(1+d)$-dimensional Minkowski spacetime $\mathbb{R ^{1+d}$: \begin{equation}{dS^{}_d} = \Big\{ x \inR^{1+d} \;;\; (x)^2 = x\cdot x = \eta^{}_{\alpha\beta} x^\alpha x^\beta = -H^{-2} \Big\}\,,\nonumber\end{equation} where $\alpha,\beta = 0,1,\ldots,d$, $\eta^{ _{\alpha\beta = diag (1,-1,\ldots,-1)$, and $H$ is a positive constant.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In the real dS$^{ _{d=4}$ case, $H$ corresponds to the Hubble constant.\footnote{For clarity, we use natural units throughout this Letter, setting the fundamental constants $c$ and $\hbar$ to unity.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The dS$^{ _d$ (relativity) group is SO$_0(1,d)$, the connected subgroup of O$(1,d)$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The corresponding Lie algebra is represented by the linear span of the Killing vectors $K_{\alpha\beta = x_\alpha\partial_\beta - x_\beta\partial_\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, none of these vectors is globally timelike, meaning that neither time nor energy can be defined globally in dS$^{_d$ spacetime \cite{dSBook .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This observation leads to our first key insight: Remark 1: An elementary system, defined as a free quantum field, resides within the global structure of dS$^{_d$ spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The absence of a globally timelike Killing vector in dS$^{ _d$ spacetime implies that ${T}$ symmetry cannot be globally defined in the conventional sense of inverting a universal time coordinate.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, at least in the sense given by Feynman-Stueckelberg, we cannot unambiguously distinguish between the matter and antimatter descriptions of the system in the global structure of dS$^{ _d$ spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Instead, ${T}$ symmetry can be understood as a local symmetry, applicable in regions where a well-defined notion of time exists, as we elaborate on now.
elements.sent-2411-017: Following the approach outlined in Ref. \cite{GibbonsHawking , let us adopt the viewpoint of a local observer (on dS$^{ _d$) in motion along the geodesic $g({x_\bullet )$ that traverses through the point ${x_\bullet =(0,\ldots,0, x^d = H^{-1 )$ (see Fig. \ref{Fig. KMS ): \begin{equation}\label{geodesic} g({x_\bullet}) =\, \Big\{ x=x(t) \; ; \; x^0 = \, H^{-1}\sinh{Ht},\nonumber {x} = \, (x^1,\ldots,x^{d-1})=0, \nonumber x^{d} = \, H^{-1}\cosh{Ht}, \quad t\inR \Big\}\,.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{geodesic} g({x_\bullet}) =\, \Big\{ x=x(t) \; ; \; x^0 = \, H^{-1}\sinh{Ht},\nonumber {x} = \, (x^1,\ldots,x^{d-1})=0, \nonumber x^{d} = \, H^{-1}\cosh{Ht}, \quad t\inR \Big\}\,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-018: Note that: (i) The chosen point $x_\bullet$ is located in the $(x^0, x^{d )$-plane.}
elements.figure-2411-001: [H] \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=.38\textheight]{analyticity.jpg} \end{center} \caption{The static patch of dS$^{}_d$, linked with an observer traveling along the geodesic $g(x_\bullet)$.} \label{FIG. KMS}
elements.sent-2411-019: The set of all ``events'' in ${\text{dS ^{ _d}$ that can be linked to the observer via the reception and emission of light signals is given by $D^{ _{g({x_\bullet ) = \big\{ x \in {\text{dS ^{ _d \; ; \; x^{d > |x^0| \big\}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: This domain is delineated by two distinct boundaries ${B ^{\pm _{g({x_\bullet ) = \big\{ x \in {\text{dS ^{ _d \; ; \; x^0 = \pm x^{d , \; x^{d >0 \big\}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These boundaries are respectively termed the ``future horizon'' and ``past horizon'' of the observer following the geodesic ${g({x_\bullet )}$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The parameter $t$ in expression \eqref{geodesic signifies the proper time of the observer moving along the geodesic ${g({x_\bullet )}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: This enables us to designate the ``time-translation group linked with ${g({x_\bullet )}$'' as the one-parameter subgroup ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$ (isomorphic to $\mathrm{SO _0(1,1)$) of the dS$^{ _d$ group $\mathrm{SO _0(1,d)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$'s transformations involve hyperbolic rotations parallel to the $(x^0, x^{d )$-plane.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: To characterize how these transformations operate on the domain $D^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$, let $x=x(t, \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x )$ represent an arbitrary point within this domain: \begin{eqnarray}\label{coordinate KMS} x(t,{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}}}) = \left \{ \begin{array}{rl} x^0 = \sqrt{H^{-2}-(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}})^2} \; \sinh{Ht}\,, \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}} = (x^1,\ldots,x^{d-1})\,, x^{d} = \sqrt{H^{-2}-(\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x}})^2} \; \cosh{Ht}\,, \quad t\in\mathbb{R}\,, \end{array}\right.\end{eqnarray}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The operation of ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$ on $x(t,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x )$ is expressed as follows: \begin{equation}{T}^{}_{g({x_\bullet})}({\tau}) \diamond x(t,\widetilde{x}) = x(t+\tau,\widetilde{x}) = x^\tau_{}\,, \quad \tau\inR \,.\nonumber\end{equation} It defines a group of isometric automorphisms within the domain $D^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The associated orbits $g^{ _{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x ({x_\bullet )$ depict separate hyperbolic branches in $D^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: These hyperbolas lie in two-dimensional plane sections that are parallel to the $(x^0, x^{d )$-plane.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Within these orbits, the singular one that defines a geodesic of dS$^{ _d$ is $g({x_\bullet ) = g^{ _{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x =\boldsymbol{0 ({x_\bullet )$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Hence, the time-translation group ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$ is particularly relevant for observers in motion on or near $g({x_\bullet)$}, provided that their proximity is significantly smaller than the dS$^{ _d$ radius of curvature.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: This brings us to the second remark: Remark 2: When a local observer fixes a time direction (proper time $t$), this act breaks the $T$ symmetry within the observable region of dS$^{_d$ spacetime (i.e., $D^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Consequently, at a given point $(x^0,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x ,x^{d )$ within this causal domain accessible to the observer, the elementary system is identified as matter.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This local identification reflects the observer's choice of time direction (in the sense given by Feynman-Stueckelberg) and aligns with the observable properties of the quantum field in this region.
elements.sent-2411-034: To support future reasoning, it is essential to add that: Remark 3-1: By establishing a preferred direction of time, the observer also implicitly defines the reversed direction of time in the corresponding mirror region $D^{_{g(-{x_\bullet ) = \big\{ x \in {\text{dS ^{ _d \; ; \; -x^{d < -|x^0| \big\}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Strictly speaking, the intrinsic time variable relevant to an observer moving along the mirror geodesic $g(-{x_\bullet )$, within $D^{ _{g(-{x_\bullet )}$, corresponds to $-t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: This arises from the fact that the associated time-translation group ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(-x_\bullet)}$ validates the relation ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(-x_\bullet) (\tau) = {\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(x_\bullet) (-\tau)$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Note that ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(-x_\bullet)}$ is derived from ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(x_\bullet)}$, for instance, through conjugation in the form ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(-x_\bullet) = R\,{\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g(x_\bullet)}\, R^{-1}$, where $R$ represents a rotation of angle $\pi$ in a plane orthogonal to the $x^0$-axis.\footnote{This characteristic serves as yet another indication of the fact that dS$^{ _d$ spacetime lacks a globally timelike Killing vector.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Remark 3-2: The two corresponding mirror regions, $D^{_{g({x_\bullet )}$ and $D^{ _{g(-{x_\bullet )}$, are causally disconnected.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Therefore, observations made in one region have no causal influence on the mirror region.
elements.sent-2411-040: Another foundational element of our reasoning lies in the imperative of analyticity within the complexified dS$^{ _d$ manifold (denoted as dS$^{(\mathbb{C ) _d$): \begin{equation}dS_d^{(C)} = \Big\{z = x+ i y \in {C}^{1+d} \;;\; (z)^2= \eta^{}_{\alpha\beta} z^\alpha z^\beta = -H^{-2} \nonumber \qquador equivalently \;;\; (x)^2 - (y)^2 = -H^{-2}, \; x\cdot y=0 \Big\}\,.\nonumber\end{equation} This analyticity requirement is crucial for the quantum field theory (QFT) describing elementary systems within the global structure of dS$^{ _d$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}dS_d^{(C)} = \Big\{z = x+ i y \in {C}^{1+d} \;;\; (z)^2= \eta^{}_{\alpha\beta} z^\alpha z^\beta = -H^{-2} \nonumber \qquador equivalently \;;\; (x)^2 - (y)^2 = -H^{-2}, \; x\cdot y=0 \Big\}\,.\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: Expanding beyond the familiar flat Minkowski spacetime to dS$^{ _d$ introduces a fundamental challenge to the QFT description of elementary systems.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The challenge lies in the absence of a well-defined spectral condition \cite{Streater .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Regardless of the chosen QFT approach to field quantization on dS$^{ _d$ spacetime, formulating requirements for locality (microcausality) and covariance is a relatively straightforward task.}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, establishing any condition on the spectrum of the ``energy'' operator proves to be a formidable challenge.
elements.sent-2411-045: As previously noted, in dS$^{ _d$ spacetime, granted that no globally timelike Killing vector exists, neither time nor energy can be globally defined.}
elements.sent-2411-046: This inherent ambiguity results in the emergence of numerous inequivalent QFTs (vacuum states) for a single field model on dS$^{ _d$ spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Each of these theories is typically linked to a specific choice of the time coordinate, leading to associated frequency splitting.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the Minkowskian framework, a notable observation highlights a direct link between the characteristics of analytic continuation in complexified spacetime and the spectral property of the model under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, in the context of $(1+d)$-dimensional Minkowski spacetime $\mathbb{R ^{1+d}$, the spectral property can be expressed as follows: for each $n>1$, the Wightman $n$-point function corresponds to the boundary value, in the distribution sense, of a function that is holomorphic within the tube $T^{+(n)}$ defined as: \begin{equation}T^{+(n)} = \Big\{ ( z^{}_1, \ldots, z^{}_n ) \in C^{n(1+d)} \;;\; \Im(z^{}_{j+1} - z^{}_j) \in {{V}}^+ \Big\}\,,\nonumber\end{equation} where $1\leqslant j \leqslant n-1$ and ${V ^\pm = \big\{y \in\mathbb{R ^{1+d \;;\; (y)^2 > 0,\; y^0 \gtrless 0 \big\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In the dS$^{ _d$ context (embedded in $\mathbb{R ^{1+d}$), a natural alternative to this property is to propose that the Wightman $n$-point function ${\cal{W _n(x_1,\ldots,x_n)$ acts as the boundary value, in the distributional sense, for a function that is holomorphic within ${\cal{T ^{+(n) = T^{+(n) \cap \big[\text{dS _d^{(\mathbb{C )}\big]^n$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Studies by Bros et al. \cite{GazeauPRL, Bros2pointfunc showed that ${\cal{T ^{+(n)}$ forms a domain and a tuboid — bounded by reals — in a manner that maintains the relevance of the concept of the ``distribution boundary value of a holomorphic function from this domain''.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Consequently, it becomes feasible to impose weak spectral condition:.
elements.sent-2411-053: Focusing on a generalized free dS$^{ _d$ field, the assertion of normal analyticity can be explicitly expressed as:.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Note that ${\cal{T ^\pm$ are referred to as the forward and backward tubes of $\text{dS _d^{(\mathbb{C )}$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This elegant resolution effectively circumvents all ambiguities in dS$^{ _d$ QFTs, which arise from the absence of a genuine spectral condition in dS$^{ _d$ spacetime \cite{GazeauPRL, Bros2pointfunc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: It leads to the derivation of ``vacua'' that, despite exhibiting thermal properties, serve as precise analogs of Minkowski vacuum representations.
elements.sent-2411-057: The latter emerges as the limit of the former when the curvature tends to zero.
elements.sent-2411-058: Now, let us examine the depicted scenario for the local observer traveling along the geodesic $g({x_\bullet )$ by acknowledging the necessity of analyticity within the complexified dS$^{ _d$ manifold (dS$^{(\mathbb{C ) _d$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The complex orbits of ${\mathfrak{T ^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$ are denoted as: \begin{equation}\label{EEE} g^{(C)}_{\widetilde{x}}({x_\bullet}) = \Big\{ z^\tau_{} = z(t+\tau,\widetilde{x}),\; \tau\inC \Big\}\,.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Note that all nonreal points linked to the complex orbits $g^{(\mathbb{C ) _{\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x ({x_\bullet )$ reside in ${\cal{T ^\pm$, the very domains of analyticity required for the QFT description of elementary systems in the global structure of dS$^{ _d$ spacetime.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In this context, due to the analytic nature of dS$^{ _d$ QFTs, a notable connection arises between points/``events'' $x=(x^0,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x ,x^{d )$ in the domain $D^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$ and their corresponding points/``events'' $(-x^0,\widetilde{\boldsymbol{x ,-x^{d )$ in the mirror region $D^{ _{g(-{x_\bullet )}$, as shown in Fig. \ref{Fig. KMS ; to see the point, with the coordinate system \eqref{coordinate KMS in consideration, setting $\Im(\tau)=\pi/H$ in \eqref{EEE suffices.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: This key property underpins the basis of our concluding remark: Remark 4-1: Returning to the elementary system discussed in Remarks 1 and 2, modeled as a free quantum field within the global structure of dS$^{_d$ spacetime, we consider the perspective of the observer in Remark 2.}
elements.sent-2411-063: When this observer identifies a matter realization of the system at a given point $(x^0, \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x , x^d) \in D^{ _{g({x_\bullet )}$, the analytic properties of dS$^{ _d$ QFTs immediately imply an equivalent description of the system at the causally disconnected mirror point $(-x^0, \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x , -x^d) \in D^{ _{g(-{x_\bullet )}$, but with the time orientation reversed (see Remark 3).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Under the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation, this mirrored realization represents the antimatter counterpart of the original matter realization at $(x^0, \widetilde{\boldsymbol{x , x^d)$.\footnote{It is crucial to underline that this observation aligns with the fact that the opposing static patches of dS$^{ _d$ spacetime are linked by a bifurcate Killing horizon, similar to the two opposing Rindler wedges in Minkowski spacetime \cite{cc .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Globally regular quantum states in dS$^{ _d$ spacetime, therefore, exhibit correlations between these opposing static patches.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In the case of a charged field, these correlations involve charge conjugation, much like the situation in Rindler spacetime, as discussed in Ref. \cite{qq .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Thus, as far as free quantum fields are concerned, we argue that while a local observer perceives a matter-antimatter asymmetry, this asymmetry is a consequence of their limited causal perspective, rather than an inherent feature of the global structure of dS$^{ _d$ spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Remark 4-2: It is crucial to underline that the kinematical perspective introduced in this Letter is not intended as a replacement for the established dynamical processes outlined by Sakharov's criteria — specifically, baryon number violation, $CP$ violation, and deviation from thermal equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-069: Instead, it serves as a complementary interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-070: We propose that traces of antimatter observed within our predominantly matter-filled Universe (or, more precisely, within the causal patch of the dS$^{_d$ Universe considered here) might indeed stem from these dynamical processes but through mechanisms distinct from the conventional view.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Although previous studies have shown that $CP$ asymmetry in particle physics alone cannot fully explain the pronounced matter-antimatter imbalance favoring matter, we suggest that, under certain conditions favoring antimatter, this $\texttt{CP$ asymmetry could account for the limited antimatter presence observed within our matter-dominated Universe (again, specifically within the dS$^{ _d$ causal patch under consideration).\footnote{So far, the best-documented examples of $CP$ violation with possible antimatter preference occur in the $B$-meson system, where direct $CP$ violation in decay processes sometimes favors antimatter states.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Additionally, if $CP$ violation is confirmed in neutrino oscillations, it could point to a fundamental asymmetry that favors antimatter (see, for instance, Refs.. \cite{111,222,333 ).}
elements.sent-2411-073: This interpretation, framed within our kinematical approach, thus explores how $CP$-violating processes might account for this subtle yet non-negligible antimatter component, despite the overwhelming dominance of matter.
elements.sent-2411-074: In conclusion, we note that the dS$^{ _{d=4}$ Universe presented here is an idealized model for real-world cosmology.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Although the cosmological constant aligns with supernova data, suggesting a dS$^{ _{d=4}$-like expansion, potential indications of dynamical dark energy, as hinted by DESI data, could challenge this exact dS$^{ _{d=4}$ form.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Furthermore, while the early Universe may have undergone accelerated expansion (e.g., during inflation), this expansion likely does not strictly conform to the dS$^{ _{d=4}$ model.}
elements.sent-2411-077: To address these challenges, it is crucial to extend our approach — particularly the key analytic insights leading to Remark 4, which, outside of the maximally symmetric cases (Minkowski, dS$^{ _d$, and Anti-dS$^{ _d$), remains largely unexplored — toward less idealized cosmological models, such as the Friedmann-Robertson-Walker spacetime, which asymptotically resembles dS$^{ _d$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: This extension would provide more robust and comprehensive results, offering insights better suited to broader and more realistic cosmological applications.
elements.section-2411-002: Discussion: Applicability of the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation in stationary vs. nonstationary spacetimes
elements.sent-2411-079: A key consideration in the traditional application of the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation for distinguishing particles from antiparticles is that it typically assumes a stationary spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-080: In such stationary spacetimes, globally well-defined concepts of time and energy enable an unambiguous identification of particles and antiparticles based on consistent energy flow and time direction.
elements.sent-2411-081: In contrast, our work extends the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation to dS$^{ _d$ spacetime, which, as we note, is not globally stationary.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Instead, we focus on the causal patches accessible to local observers within dS$^{ _d$ spacetime, where a well-defined notion of time exists.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In this context, by considering the analytic continuation in the complexified dS$^{ _d$ spacetime (denoted dS$^{(\mathbb{C ) _d$), we exploit its inherent symmetry and analyticity to establish a ``mirror'' relationship between causally disconnected regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This structure allows us to interpret the matter manifestation of an elementary system (a globally defined free quantum field) at a given point in one causal patch as having a corresponding antimatter manifestation at the mirror point in a causally disconnected region, where time flows in the opposite direction.
elements.sent-2411-085: The mirror point, mathematically defined by extending coordinates into the complex plane, directly represents the antimatter counterpart of the original matter realization.
elements.sent-2411-086: Thus, our approach for identifying antiparticles in dS$^{ _d$ spacetime does not rely on global stationarity.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Instead, it leverages the unique analytic continuation and causal structure of dS$^{ _d$, enabling a local, observer-dependent adaptation of the Feynman-Stueckelberg interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Here, particle-antiparticle distinctions are defined within individual causal patches, rather than globally.
elements.sent-2411-089: In summary, our analysis modifies the Feynman-Stueckelberg framework to accommodate the nonstationary nature of dS$^{ _d$ spacetime by making use of its causal and analytic structure.}
elements.sent-2411-090: This yields a region-specific approach to particle-antiparticle identification, which does not depend on global stationarity.
